bibliographic.citation: Christine de Pizan, The Epistle of Othea to Hector or The Boke of Knyghthode, translated from the French by Stephen Scrope, edited from a manuscript in the library of the Marquis of Bath by George F. Warner (London: J. B. Nichols and Sons, 1904).
bibliographic.title: The Epistle of Othea to Hector or The Boke of Knyghthode

entities.description: George F. Warner's first printed edition of Scrope's English text, based on Longleat House MS 253 and furnished with an introduction, French comparison readings from Harley MS 4431, glossary, and index.
entities.label: Warner, The Epistle of Othea to Hector (1904)
notes: The English work occupies printed pp. 1-114; CME00015 encodes that text and the title page.
Warner recognized the missing Longleat leaf and quire and used brackets and notes for comparison or supplied matter rather than silently claiming a complete base witness.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1904; the Internet Archive item marks it NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and the current U-M item applies No Copyright-United States. This is not a worldwide rights statement.
rights.component: Warner's 1904 edition, including the Middle English transcription and historical editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: United States

access.accessMethod: Submit a research enquiry and reader registration; appointments, fees, and a letter of introduction for manuscripts apply.
No item-level public facsimile was found. Submit Longleat's image request form or email the Collections team about complete existing photography.
access.format: No public image deliverable identified; requestable research images
notes: The public page says manuscript microfilm PDFs may sometimes be purchased for research and separately offers charged image requests; it exposes no MS 253 viewer or downloadable complete codex.
This dated negative is limited to public sources checked on 11 July 2026 and does not assert that Longleat holds no internal photography.

entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century English parchment codex of 95 folios containing Scrope's Epistle on ff. 2r-75v and The Tree of Love on ff. 76r-95v. The Epistle lacks a leaf after f. 9v and a quire after f. 34v and retains unfilled spaces for rubrics, initials, Latin quotations, and miniatures.

holding.notes: The current public access page supplies enquiry, image-request, and reading-room procedures but no item-level record or public facsimile.

entities.label: Longleat House MS 253

physicalDescription.format: Codex in nineteenth-century dark brown leather binding
notes: The present fol. 1 is an old parchment cover carrying a title.
The work's documentary, nonprofessional hand and copying errors do not support Warner's historical suggestion that this was the original Fastolf copy.

access.accessMethod: Arrange research access through the Old Library Rare Books Reading Room.
access.entityLabel: St John's College, Cambridge, MS H.5

access.accessMethod: No current complete viewer was found; request private research copies or high-resolution images from Special Collections.
access.entityLabel: St John's College, Cambridge, MS H.5
access.format: No public complete image deliverable identified; reproductions requestable
notes: A legacy selected-image page cited by older scholarship now returns not found, and the former item catalog route redirects to generic highlights.
The current Library page explicitly offers reading copies for private research and high-resolution publication images by request.

entities.description: The most complete of the three surviving English witnesses and the base manuscript for Schieberle 2020. A professional mid-fifteenth-century parchment copy, probably written by Ricardus Franciscus, with a complete visual program and dedication to Humphrey Stafford; one leaf is missing after f. 55v.
entities.label: St John's College, Cambridge, MS H.5

physicalDescription.format: Illuminated codex containing the Epistle, with later flyleaf additions
notes: Six illuminations survive in a complete planned decorative program.

access.accessMethod: Open the official Morgan manuscript record.
No complete public physical-object surrogate was found; request complete photography through Morgan Imaging and Rights.
access.format: No public complete image deliverable identified; reproduction order route available
notes: The official selected viewer exposes 14 leaves from a 320-folio codex; the Morgan's Digital Facsimiles listing does not include M.775.
This dated negative is limited to public routes checked on 11 July 2026 and does not assert that complete institutional photography is unavailable by request.

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: Third or fourth quarter of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A professional mid-fifteenth-century English parchment composite of 320 folios. Scrope's acephalous Epistle occupies ff. 200r-274v, is addressed to an unnamed princess, and was later appropriated by Sir John Astley; some miniatures are missing or removed.
entities.label: Morgan Library & Museum, MS M.775

physicalDescription.format: Composite codex in sixteenth-century stamped calf binding

entities.description: A French parchment manuscript associated with Sir John Fastolf and the Master of Sir John Fastolf. Christine's Epistre Othea occupies ff. 24r-92v. It is textually close to Scrope's lost French source but is not established as his direct physical exemplar.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 570

notes: Digital Bodleian describes French origin and Middle French language and associates the illumination with the Master of Sir John Fastolf.

entities.description: Christine de Pizan's lavish presentation collection for Queen Isabeau, produced in Paris around 1413-1414 and now bound in two volumes. The French Epistre Othea occupies ff. 95r-141v and is illustrated by 101 miniatures. Warner uses this witness as French comparison H.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 4431

physicalDescription.extent: A complete published sequence numbered 001r-399v; the project describes 398 folios and thirty works
physicalDescription.format: Lavishly illuminated composite collection, now bound as two volumes
notes: The Edinburgh project reports 132 miniatures across the manuscript.

entities.description: The French source work composed around 1400, designed as one hundred texte, glose, and allegorie chapters. Scrope's Middle English Epistle is a translation of this work; no surviving manuscript is equated with the abstract source work or with Scrope's lost exemplar.
entities.label: Christine de Pizan's French Epistre Othea

reviewNotes: Scrope's Middle English translation, Christine's French source work, the three English witnesses, the French comparison witnesses, Warner and Schieberle's editions, image surrogates, the U-M encoding, and the repository artifact are distinct layers.
The direct French exemplar used by Scrope has not survived. Bodleian Laud Misc. 570 is a sister manuscript probably copied from the same lost French source, not a demonstrated physical exemplar.
Warner's edition follows incomplete Longleat MS 253 and uses Harley MS 4431 as French comparison H; it does not transcribe Harley as its English base.
Schieberle's edition uses St John's H.5 as base and compares Morgan M.775 and Longleat 253; it is collateral scholarship, not the source of CME00015.

Completeness claims for cached image bundles are provider-inventory claims and are qualified against the physical object: Morgan and Bodleian expose selected subsets; Harley exposes the complete published 001r-399v leaf sequence but no covers, boards, spine, or binding views.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00015 XML copies the University of Michigan CME encoding of the title page and printed pp. 1-114 of George F. Warner's 1904 edition of Stephen Scrope's Middle English Epistle of Othea. Warner transcribed the incomplete Longleat House MS 253, ff. 2r-75v, and collated French readings against British Library Harley MS 4431. The conceptual translation has one hundred chapters, but Longleat has lost a leaf after f. 9v and a quire after f. 34v. Misty Schieberle's 2020 edition instead uses St John's College MS H.5 as base and collates Morgan MS M.775 and Longleat, with Bodleian Laud Misc. 570 among the French comparison sources. Complete PDFs of Warner and Schieberle are cached; provider-complete selected-image bundles are cached for Morgan and Bodleian, and a complete 001r-399v published leaf sequence is cached privately for Harley. No complete public physical-object facsimile was found for Longleat, St John's, Morgan, or Bodleian.

catalogingSubject.label: The Epistle of Othea
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Stephen Scrope's Middle English translation of Christine de Pizan's Epistre Othea, made around 1440, including Scrope's prose dedication to Sir John Fastolf and the one-hundred-chapter texte, glose, and allegorie design. CME00015 represents the incomplete Longleat House MS 253 form through Warner's 1904 edition. The cataloged work is not Christine's French source work, any physical manuscript, Warner's printed edition, Schieberle's modern edition, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML.

contentParts.label: Scrope's dedication to Sir John Fastolf
notes: The approximate Longleat subrange is inferred from the edition and manuscript milestones; the securely established complete work locus is ff. 2r-75v.
contentParts.summary: A prose dedicatory preface identifying Scrope's translation and presenting it to his stepfather, Sir John Fastolf.

contentParts.label: One hundred Othea chapters

locators.value: approximately ff. 5r-75v, with physical losses after ff. 9v and 34v

notes: The item count describes the work's designed extent; only 90 chapter units are represented at least in part by the incomplete Longleat-derived text.
contentParts.summary: The designed sequence of one hundred mythological instruction units, each normally comprising a verse texte, a prose glose, and a prose allegorie.

assertion.notes: The date belongs to Scrope's act of translation, not automatically to any extant witness.
Warner states that he prints the English text for the first time.

dateStatements.display: First printed in 1904 in George F. Warner's edition

extent.description: Conceptually, one prose dedication and one hundred mixed-form chapters. The incomplete Longleat-derived XML represents 89 numbered chapter DIV2 elements, one unnumbered surviving chapter-49 fragment, one lacuna placeholder, 90 commentary DIV3 elements, 88 allegorical-interpretation DIV3 elements, 481 verse L elements outside notes, 188 BODY P elements, 492 BODY NOTE elements, 115 PB elements, and 72 MILESTONE elements. The 481-line count is an encoding metric for surviving represented verse, not the complete work's canonical verse extent.

formStatements.description: A prose Fastolf dedication followed by chapters that combine a verse texte with prose glose and prose allegorie.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Warner supplies some Latin authorities in brackets from comparison material where Longleat left spaces; those editorial supplies are not treated as a second intrinsic language of the Longleat-form work record.

The work record describes Scrope's translation, while the lineage manifest models the three English witnesses, two French comparison witnesses, editions, digital encoding, repository artifact, and image surrogates separately.
Longleat's material losses are not converted into a shorter conceptual work extent.
Warner's supplied Latin readings and French comparison notes belong to the edition layer and are not silently attributed to the incomplete Longleat manuscript.
The adjacent Tree of Love in Longleat MS 253, Warner's introduction/glossary/index, and Schieberle's separately edited Lytle Bibell of Knyghthod are outside the cataloging boundary.

next_steps: Compare Scrope's readings against the full BI-family apparatus in Schieberle and specialist French editions.
Keep Bodleian Laud Misc. 570 as a sister manuscript associated with Fastolf, not a direct-exemplar claim.
Do not infer physical descent from textual closeness alone.
openQuestions.question: Can Scrope's lost French exemplar be identified more precisely within the BI manuscript family?

next_steps: Consult documentary studies of Scrope and Fastolf's household around 1440.
Distinguish the translation's date and place from the later production of each extant witness.
Retain England as cultural origin and leave exact place unknown until directly supported.
openQuestions.question: Where exactly did Scrope make the translation, and for what initial production context?

next_steps: Request complete photography of Longleat House MS 253, including binding, endleaves, blanks, all 95 folios, and the adjacent Tree of Love.
Map the Epistle securely to ff. 2r-75v and then refine internal preface/chapter boundaries from the images.
Confirm retention and reuse terms before caching any supplied files.
openQuestions.question: Can the exact Longleat folio boundary between Scrope's Fastolf preface and chapter 1 be verified from complete manuscript images?

assertion.notes: Scrope's English translation and Fastolf dedication support an English cultural origin, but the inspected evidence does not establish an exact place of translation.
This source authorship is distinct from responsibility for Scrope's English wording and Fastolf dedication.

scope.description: Authorship of the French work translated throughout Scrope's Middle English version
The 1904 edition used by the U-M encoding
Longleat House MS 253, fols. 2r-75v, as represented on printed pp. 1-114

subjects.label: Classical mythology interpreted through Christian moral theology

summaries.value: In a prose dedication, Stephen Scrope offers Sir John Fastolf his Middle English translation of Christine de Pizan's Epistre Othea. Othea, figured as a goddess of prudence, instructs the young Hector through one hundred concise mythological exempla. Each chapter normally joins a verse texte to prose moral explanation and Christian allegory, turning stories of classical gods, heroes, and the Trojan War into counsel on chivalry, governance, virtues and vices, spiritual conduct, and the authority of learned women.

The conceptual work has one hundred chapters, but the Longleat witness represented by Warner and CME00015 is materially incomplete: it lacks a leaf after fol. 9v and a quire after fol. 34v. The encoding contains 89 numbered chapters, the surviving tail of chapter 49 as an unnumbered division, and an explicit lacuna; it must not be used to infer a ninety-chapter original.

assertion.notes: The short form is used by modern scholarship for Scrope's translation and avoids treating Warner's two-part title as the only work title.

notes: Warner's title-page form.
titles.value: The Epistle of Othea to Hector

notes: The alternate title printed after OR on Warner's title page and preserved in the CME encoding.
